perturbation.original_text: 8.1 Each Party shall at all times indemnify and hold harmless the other, its directors, officers, employees and any others for whom it may be responsible in law, from and against all claims, including claims made by the indemnifying Party's personnel under worker's compensation legislation, demands, awards, judgments, actions and proceedings by whomsoever made, brought or prosecuted in respect of loss of, damage to or destruction of property (including loss or damage sustained by the indemnifying party) or personal injury including death and from and against any and all loss or, damage to or destruction of property, expenses and costs (including legal fees and disbursements) suffered or incurred by the indemnifying Party, its directors, officers, employees and any others for whom it may be responsible in law, arising out of or in any way connected with the indemnifying Party, its directors, officers, employees and any others for whom it may be responsible in law, arising out of or in any way connected with the indemnifying Party provision of Services under this Agreement and whether or not caused by the indemnifying Party's negligence. Loss or damage sustained by the indemnifying Party shall also include loss as a result of loss of use.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.1 Each Party shall at all times indemnify and hold harmless the other, its directors, officers, employees and any others for whom it may be responsible in law, from and against all claims, including claims made by the indemnifying Party's personnel under worker's compensation legislation, demands, awards, judgments, actions and proceedings by whomsoever made, brought or prosecuted in respect of loss of, damage to or destruction of property (including loss or damage sustained by the indemnifying party) or personal injury including death and from and against any and all loss or, damage to or destruction of property, expenses and costs (including legal fees and disbursements) suffered or incurred by the indemnifying Party, its directors, officers, employees and any others for whom it may be responsible in law, arising out of or in any way connected with the indemnifying Party, its directors, officers, employees and any others for whom it may be responsible in law, arising out of or in any way connected with the indemnifying Party provision of Services under this Agreement and whether or not caused by the indemnifying Party's negligence. Harm sustained by the indemnifying Party shall also include emotional distress.
perturbation.explanation: By changing “Loss or damage sustained by the indemnifying Party shall also include loss as a result of loss of use” to “Harm sustained by the indemnifying Party shall also include emotional distress”, creates an in-text contradiction as Section 8.1 initially pertains to financial and physical harm or loss (property damage, personal injury, loss of use of property), which typically falls under the scope of indemnification in commercial agreements. Inserting emotional distress as a type of 'Harm' sustained introduces a new category of non-economic damages, which is typically harder to quantify and not commonly included in indemnification clauses. This conflicts with the original intent and creates uncertainty in enforcement, creating a contradiction because 'Harm' now incorporates a broader range of damages than initially implied.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8.1 Each Party shall at all times indemnify and hold harmless the other, its directors, officers, employees and any others for whom it may be responsible in law, from and against all claims, including claims made by the indemnifying Party's personnel under worker's compensation legislation, demands, awards, judgments, actions and proceedings by whomsoever made, brought or prosecuted in respect of loss of, damage to or destruction of property (including loss or damage sustained by the indemnifying party) or personal injury including death and from and against any and all loss or, damage to or destruction of property, expenses and costs (including legal fees and disbursements) suffered or incurred by the indemnifying Party, its directors, officers, employees and any others for whom it may be responsible in law, arising out of or in any way connected with the indemnifying Party, its directors, officers, employees and any others for whom it may be responsible in law, arising out of or in any way connected with the indemnifying Party provision of Services under this Agreement and whether or not caused by the indemnifying Party's negligence. Harm sustained by the indemnifying Party shall also include emotional distress.
perturbation.justification: The change from "Loss or damage sustained by the indemnifying Party shall also include loss as a result of loss of use" to "Harm sustained by the indemnifying Party shall also include emotional distress" introduces a new category of non-economic damages (emotional distress) into an indemnification clause that previously focused on quantifiable financial and physical losses (property damage, personal injury, loss of use). This creates a contradiction because the original section 8.1 sets out the types of harms that are being considered which seem to be financial and physical (loss, damage, destruction of property, personal injury including death) then changes at the end to include 'emotional distress'. Indemnification clauses typically cover direct and reasonably foreseeable damages, making the inclusion of emotional distress a significant departure. This creates uncertainty and potential unenforceability, as emotional distress is subjective and harder to quantify, directly contradicting the original intent and scope of the indemnification outlined in section 8.1.

perturbation.original_text: 9.1 Each Party shall purchase and maintain, at its own expense, the following insurance coverages: (a) cargo liability insurance, subject to a combined single limit of not less than One Hundred Thousand dollars ($100,000.00) inclusive per occurrence. The other Party shall be named as an additional insured and the policy shall contain a cross liability clause; (b) automobile, non-owned automobile, fleet, comprehensive general, public and property liability insurance with a limit of not less than Two Million dollars ($2,000,000.00) inclusive of bodily injury and property damage for any one occurrence arising out of one (1) cause. The policy shall cover all non-air operations, non-owned automobile, contractual liability and liability specifically assumed under this Agreement. The other party shall be named as an additional insured and the policy shall contain a cross liability clause;
perturbation.changed_text: 9.1 Each Party shall purchase and maintain, at its own expense, the following insurance coverages: (a) cargo liability insurance, subject to a combined single limit of not less than One Hundred Thousand dollars ($100,000.00) inclusive per occurrence. The other Party shall be named as an additional insured and the policy shall contain a cross liability clause; (b) automobile, non-owned automobile, fleet, comprehensive general, public and property liability insurance with a limit of not less than Two Million dollars ($2,000,000.00) inclusive of bodily injury and property damage for any one occurrence arising out of one (1) cause. The policy shall cover all non-air operations, non-owned automobile, contractual liability and liability specifically assumed under this Agreement. The other party shall be designated as an additional insured and the policy shall contain a cross liability clause;
perturbation.explanation: In Section 9.1 (b), changing “named as an additional insured” to “designated as an additional insured” introduces inconsistent terminology without defining if 'named' and 'designated' carry the same legal weight. 'Named' has a more standard meaning in insurance, implying explicit inclusion in the policy. 'Designated' is ambiguous, potentially leading to disputes over whether the party is actually covered. This creates a contradiction, because the intention is to provide insurance and additional coverage, but now there is uncertantenty if 'designated' is actually covering the parties.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9.1 Each Party shall purchase and maintain, at its own expense, the following insurance coverages: (a) cargo liability insurance, subject to a combined single limit of not less than One Hundred Thousand dollars ($100,000.00) inclusive per occurrence. The other Party shall be named as an additional insured and the policy shall contain a cross liability clause; (b) automobile, non-owned automobile, fleet, comprehensive general, public and property liability insurance with a limit of not less than Two Million dollars ($2,000,000.00) inclusive of bodily injury and property damage for any one occurrence arising out of one (1) cause. The policy shall cover all non-air operations, non-owned automobile, contractual liability and liability specifically assumed under this Agreement. The other party shall be designated as an additional insured and the policy shall contain a cross liability clause;
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'named as an additional insured' to 'designated as an additional insured' introduces ambiguity regarding the scope of insurance coverage. While both sections aim to provide insurance, the term 'designated' lacks the established legal meaning of 'named' in insurance contexts. This ambiguity directly contradicts the intention of providing clear and enforceable insurance coverage, potentially leading to disputes about whether the other party is actually covered, which creates uncertainty where the contract expects it.

perturbation.original_text: 17.1 For the purposes of this Agreement, the following shall constitute events of default by a Party (hereinafter referred to as "Events of Default"): (a) if a petition is filed against it under any applicable bankruptcy legislation and is not withdrawn or dismissed within sixty (60) days thereafter; (b) if a resolution is passed by it respecting the sale of all or substantially all of its assets, or an order for the winding up of its business is made, or it otherwise agrees to make a bulk sale of it's assets; (c) if it ceases or threatens to cease to carry on its business;
perturbation.changed_text: 17.1 For the purposes of this Agreement, the following shall constitute events of default by a Party (hereinafter referred to as "Breaches of Contract"): (a) if a petition is filed against it under any applicable bankruptcy legislation and is not withdrawn or dismissed within sixty (60) days thereafter; (b) if a resolution is passed by it respecting the sale of all or substantially all of its assets, or an order for the winding up of its business is made, or it otherwise agrees to make a bulk sale of it's assets; (c) if it ceases or threatens to cease to carry on its business;
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Events of Default' to 'Breaches of Contract' in Section 17.1, where 'Events of Default' is a defined term within the contract, the original agreement delineates specific occurrences that automatically trigger defined consequences. By altering this term to 'Breaches of Contract', the agreement generates ambiguity. 'Breaches of Contract' typically necessitate establishing a violation of a specific contract term, whereas 'Events of Default', in the unaltered document, are predefined triggers irrespective of direct contractual violation. This terminological misalignment creates uncertainty regarding enforcement, as the process for identifying and addressing 'Breaches of Contract' requires interpretation and proof of direct violation, a step not required for the originally defined 'Events of Default'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 17.2 Upon the occurrence of an Event of Default and in addition to any rights or remedies available to it under this Agreement or at law or in equity, the Party not in default may exercise any or all of the following remedies: (a) terminate this Agreement, upon giving one hundred and twenty (120) days written notice, otherwise upon written notice with respect to 17.1 (g) and (h); (b) recover from the defaulting Party any and all monies then due and to become due; and (c) take possession, immediately, without demand or notice, without any court order or other process of law, any and all of its property (including bags and containers) and Freight received by the defaulting Party under this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'Events of Default' with 'Breaches of Contract' in the definition section (17.1). The contradicted text (17.2) outlines remedies specifically triggered by 'Events of Default'. By changing the defined term, the agreement creates a contradiction because the remedies outlined in 17.2 are no longer clearly applicable, as 'Breaches of Contract' carries a different legal meaning and requires a different standard of proof than the originally defined 'Events of Default'. This introduces ambiguity and potential unenforceability, creating a strong in-text contradiction.
